reason: The item cleanly tests admissible inference boundaries between gas yield, peak temperature, and SOC sensitivity, with correct choices tightly anchored to the stated trend reversals and ratio change. Decoys are mostly single-fault and engineering-plausible (energy-from-gas, geometry-only attribution, gas-volume-determines-temperature), producing good expert discrimination. Minor risk remains that B can be seen as leaning on an interpretive definition of “sensitivity” from a ratio statement without explicit absolute deltas, slightly softening boundary tightness and discriminative sharpness.

The item cleanly tests SOC-conditional reasoning about ignition hazard using LEL/UEL direction changes and explicitly links the reversal to composition, which supports tight admissibility boundaries. Decoys are plausible and mainly fail by a single identifiable overgeneralization/mechanism-substitution move, yielding good discrimination, though some options (A/E) risk inviting simplistic “higher limit = safer/more hazardous” heuristics without explicitly constraining other hazard determinants (e.g., ignition energy, mixing/ventilation).

The prompt cleanly forces a mechanistic boundary-aware judgment on two proxy claims: (i) gas/Ah vs thermal severity and (ii) nitrogen pressure data vs air-design conservatism, using explicit cross-format and air–nitrogen contrasts that discourage naive monotonic assumptions. Diagnostic value is high because an expert must reconcile decoupled observables (gas vs temperature; inert vs oxidizing environment) and correctly attribute the air pressure increase to post-ejection oxidation. Minor quality limits come from SHORT_ANSWER grading latitude (risk of partially correct mechanistic narratives) and the absence of explicit quantitative thresholds for what counts as a “reliable proxy,” which can slightly broaden acceptable interpretations.
